steps.text: The sun has barely risen when Jessie Reese and the researchers from Bird Conservancy of the Rockies pull off onto a dirt track in the Chihuahuan Desert.
It's their ninth consecutive day of searching for three key grassland bird species facing significant declines in their populations.
The yellow-throated and rusty-necked Chestnut-collared Longspur, black-masked Horned Lark, and the brown-streaked Baird's Sparrow have each lost around 65 percent of their populations since 1970.
Reese started out studying birds in a similar fashion — with her head out of a car window, more than 15 years ago.

html:
The sun has barely risen when Jessie Reese and the researchers from Bird Conservancy of the Rockies pull off onto a dirt track in the Chihuahuan Desert.  

It’s their ninth consecutive day of searching for three key grassland bird species facing significant declines in their populations.

The yellow-throated and rusty-necked Chestnut-collared Longspur, black-masked Horned Lark, and the brown-streaked Baird’s Sparrow have each lost around 65 percent of their populations since 1970.

The team of biologists followed these tiny songbirds to their New Mexico wintering grounds in order to study and collect data that could help to explain the threats these birds are facing and where.

As they near their worksite, Reese and avian ecologists, Nick Haddad and Kylie Lamoree, poke their heads out of the truck windows, carefully scanning the landscape for the birds while the rest of crew drives closely behind them.

Reese started out studying birds in a similar fashion — with her head out of a car window, more than 15 years ago.

She became interested in researching birds after taking a class at James Madison University where her professor would load students up into a 12-passenger van at 7 a.m. and drive around the county looking at birds.

"My eyes were open to an entire world that I had no idea existed," she said. comment

Across North America, grasslands and the birds that depend on them are rapidly vanishing.

In the last half-century, grassland birds have lost over half of their populations, declining at a rate of over 40 percentthe 2025 State of the Birds Report. And despite ongoing efforts by conservationists and landowners across the Great Plains to restore habitat and curb losses, the declines are accelerating, according to a new study in the journal Science.

The birds signal a larger issue — the health of the Great Plains region is steadily worsening, which if neglected, could result in a modern day Dust Bowl.

“Grasslands are the lifeblood of the central part of the U.S. and if they disappear, then this landscape will be drastically altered.” Brandt Ryder, chief conservation scientist at Bird Conservancy of the Rockies, said.

Reese, who has led Bird Conservancy’s Migratory Ecology Program since January 2025, is one of many conservation professionals racing to figure out how to stall a looming extinction crisis.

While Bird Conservancy has a long history of working on grassland birds, in 2020, it began developing its Migratory Ecology Program, which is designed to study grassland bird migration across the Great Plains and Chihuahuan Desert. For the past three winters, field crews from Bird Conservancy of the Rockies have traveled to New Mexico to tag and monitor grassland birds on their non-breeding grounds using Motus technology.

The Motus Wildlife Tracking System, started in 2014 by Birds Canada, is a global collaborative network of over 2,000 automated telemetry stations that allows researchers to study the movements of small animals tagged with radio transmitters.

“Bird Conservancy has really helped the Motus Network grow in the Great Plains and the Chihuahuan Desert,” Reese said. In the past five years, the organization has installed over 100 radio towers and tagged almost 400 grassland birds.

At the time the Migratory Ecology Program was started, there was a significant lack of Motus stations in the Great Plains and longstanding research on grassland birds had mostly been conducted on the breeding grounds.

Because of this, the full life cycles of the species were an enigma. Scientists knew little about grassland birds’ long distance movements, migratory timing, stopover locations or survival along the Central Flyway. This posed significant challenges when trying to create effective and long-lasting conservation practices for declining species.

But over time as Bird Conservancy has developed and implemented Motus infrastructure in the breeding and non-breeding grounds, that knowledge gap has started to close. With data from the Migratory Ecology Program, the organization has started to assess where to focus conservation efforts and how to strategically recover target species.

This is a Chestnut-collared Longspur.

It’s one of the key species Bird Conservancy is targeting for tagging along with the Baird’s Sparrow, Sprague’s Pipit, Thick-Billed Longspur, Grasshopper Sparrow, Horned Lark.

According to the North American Breeding Bird Survey, it is estimated that the Chestnut-collared Longspur population has declined 73 percent since 1967.

Similar to most declining grassland birds, significant degradation and loss of breeding habitat has caused the species to disappear from much of its historical range.

“The work that we’re doing is important for conservation of birds like the Chestnut-collared Longspur because before we can go out and try to conserve or better manage habitat, we have to understand what is causing these bird populations to decline in the first place,” Reese said.

After successfully catching the bird, Reese and the field crew recorded data on its age, sex, weight, muscle and fat content, wings, tail and feathers.

This Chestnut-collared Longspur is a young adult male, which the team determined by examining the feather colors.

Once data on its physical measurements was collected, the technicians attached a Motus tag that sits like a tiny, lightweight backpack between the bird’s wings.  

From now on, as this Chestnut moves across North America throughout its migratory journey, Motus stations will record any time it flies within 10 to 15 kilometers of the towers and mark the detection on a map.

Equipped with a tag and ready for monitoring, Reese safely released the bird back into its habitat.

“They typically have no problem flying — yep, typically have no problem flying away.”

Since 19TK, it’s breeding range has reduced by TK percent.

A biome in collapse

Despite being among the most endangered ecosystems in the U.S., grasslands are the least protected.

According to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, 62 percent of the North American grassland biome has been lost due to agricultural conversion, woody-plant invasion, drought and other climate change-related stressors. World Wildlife Fund estimates that 1 to 2 million acres of grasslands continue to be lost each year.

The Great Plains ecosystem is as important for birds as it is for the entire United States’ population.

Roughly one-fourth of all U.S. crops and half of the country’s beef cattle are grown in the Great Plains. Additionally, healthy grasslands provide vital ecosystem services such as carbon sequestration, pollinator habitat, improved soil quality and moisture retention.

As climate change triggers rising temperatures, shifts in precipitation patterns and more frequent extreme weather events such as drought and wildfires, the loss of healthy grasslands for birds and agriculture falls into an intensifying spiral.

“Grasslands are essential for the economic livelihoods of millions of people across this geography and so we have all these reasons why we need to protect and restore them,” Ryder said.

In the Northern Great Plains, agricultural conversion is driving the loss of grasslands at a rate of [TK: can I calculate this rate somehow?].

Over the past [TK] years, [#TK] amount of grasslands were converted to cropland in the Northern Great Plain states, which includes North Dakota, South Dakota, Montana, Wyoming and Nebraska, [according to our analysis…?].  

In the Southern and Central Great Plain states, another threat is driving the loss of grasslands. Over the past few decades, the encroachment of woody plants, such as red cedar and mesquite trees, has taken over the landscape.

Throughout history, indigenous land practices and free roaming bison helped to maintain the grassland biome. However, decades of fire suppression, the near elimination of bison on the land, as well as the creation of windbreaks for agricultural fields, allowed the trees to grow rampant.

“The reality is that we can't restore all of the grassland that's been lost in North America,” Reese said. “So we really need to understand where we should be doing conservation work — are there places that are going to be more beneficial where we'll get a bigger bang for our conservation buck?”

*******

A recent trend in duck populations might indicate an even more concerning future for grassland birds, as Bird Conservancy models created using data from Motus and various other sources reveal the Prairie Pothole Region to be of critical importance for fading species such as the Baird’s Sparrow.

The Prairie Pothole Region consists of a mixture of depressional wetlands and prairie habitat that are used as breeding grounds for up to 50 percent of North America’s duck and waterfowl populations, as well as several grassland species.

In contrast to grassland birds, wetland and waterfowl species populations have been on an upward trend since 1970, as a result of successful conservation movements and environmental policies like the Clean Air and Water Acts, Farm Bill and North American Waterfowl Management Plan.  

However, after hitting record high levels around 2016, duck populations have been in a fairly steady decline due to severe, widespread drought across the prairie potholes.

Michael Brasher, senior waterfowl scientist for Ducks Unlimited, said that this is not entirely abnormal. The fluctuations in duck populations are closely tied to the health of the area, which is naturally subjected to periodic drought.

However, at the same time, the Prairie Pothole Region — like the rest of the Great Plains — is shrinking. It is currently estimated that up to 60 percent of the Prairie Pothole wetlands have been altered, drained or destroyed due to increased agricultural and commercial development.

In addition, the Trump administration has pushed forward weakening legacy environmental protection laws and policies — such as Clean Water, Clean Air, Endangered Species and National Environmental Protection acts — and climate change is expected to increase the frequency and severity of drought.

So while duck populations are eventually expected to increase again, conservationists worry that the species won’t be able to rebound to their former levels.

“Despite still being a conservation success story, we have probably more risks now to duck habitats and duck populations than we have had in a long time,” Brasher said.

Climate work under attack

About 60 to 70 percent of Bird Conservancy’s funding comes through federal sources.

Last year, the organization felt the impacts of the Trump administration attacks on climate change and the environment through the termination of a $1.3 million Department of the Interior grant, the freezing of another significant portion of funds and the 43-day government shutdown.  

The majority of the lost funding was for a program called the Sustainable Grazing Network Program. Under this program Bird Conservancy works with private landowners down in Mexico on long term conservation projects on their properties to [something something] non-breeding grounds. With that, the monitoring work on grassland birds being done on those properties also lost funding.

The other programs that endured cuts included a database called the Avian Conservation Assessment Database — a tool used to inform conservation decision making at all kinds of different jurisdictional levels — and the Integrated Monitoring of Bird Conservation Region program. This program is [TK].

“The justification for these cuts isn’t totally clear, but the message was unmistakable: our commitment to a vision where everyone has access to and feels welcome in nature has somehow become controversial,” Bird Conservancy executive director Tammy VerCouteren and communications director Jacob Job wrote in Issue 65 of Primary Source, the organization’s bi-annual magazine.

But despite these hardships, Bird Conservancy managed to weather the storm.

[TK: no layoffs, still continuing to do all the work they do, largest fundraising year ever]

“Are we going into an extinction crisis? Well, if we don’t have conservation professionals and we don’t invest in it, then year, we likely will,” Ryder said.

‘What’s good for the bird is good for the herd.’

While ranching and farming have contributed to the loss of grasslands, it is also a crucial part of the solution moving forward.

Historically, grassland birds evolved on the landscape with large ungulates such as bison. Cattle serve the same purpose in terms of the impact that they have on the vegetation and habitat for grassland birds.

“We have pithy statements like, ‘what’s good for the bird is good for the herd’,” Ryder said.

Across the Great Plains, Bird Conservancy and several other conservation organizations are working with ranchers and farmers to implement management strategies that help birds and the land.

Through programs like Bird Conservancy’s Sustainable Grazing Network, Audubon’s Conservation Ranching Program and the U.S. Department of Agriculture’s Conservation Reserve Program, thousands of ranchers and farmers across the continent have…

“There are a bunch of different flavors of programs that highlight these conservation successes,” Ryder said. “Whether Audubon Conservation Ranching or Bird Conservancy of the Rockies, we’re out there working side by side with the ranching community to help them and help us at the same time.”

Sarah Sortum — the fourth generation co-owner of Switzer Ranch, in Loup County, Nebraska — began implementing bird conservation practices on her land about 15 years ago. The spread of red cedar had become a big issue for her family’s cattle operations and for their bird watching tourism business.

To address this, Sortum mechanically removed the trees followed by a period of prescribed burning, in an effort to bring back and maintain the birds’ prairie habitat.

<iframe title="vimeo-player" src="https://player.vimeo.com/video/696741118?h=7832bddb5b" width="640" height="360" frameborder="0" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allow="autoplay; fullscreen; picture-in-picture; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; web-share"   allowfullscreen></iframe>

After a while, Sortum started to notice that improving the health of the ecosystem didn’t only benefit the birds, but it also benefited their cattle operation as well as it increased the space and forage available for grazing.

“I really see the birds on our place as my barometer for our overall resource health,” Sortum said. “For ​​farmers and ranchers — like any other family business — your hope and dream is that generations after you will benefit from what you've done and will be able to continue with the same opportunities. If we don't have a healthy resource, then that's going to be very challenging.”

Because of this, implementing bird conservation on the land is a no brainer to Sortum.

“I mean, can you imagine a world without birds? Come on. They add a quality to life that just makes everything more special,” she said.

FIRST DRAFT

HED: Bye Bye Birdies

DEK: Six years after scientists reported that billions of birds vanished from the North American landscape since 1970, conservationists are still racing to reverse catastrophic losses that show no sign of slowing down.

Sections:

  1. Scene setter - Chihuahuan Desert, New Mexico
  2. Data/Science - What is happening to the birds? Why is this happening?
  3. Importance - Bigger picture of bird loss
  4. Action Pt. 1 - What are people doing about it: scientists
  5. Challenges - Counter-efforts/threats to conservation work
  6. Who cares? - Stakeholders and stakes
  7. Action Pt. 2 - What are people doing about it: conservation programs
  8. Future - What’s to come?

Scene setter

It is 5:30 a.m. on a cold January day in Las Cruces, New Mexico.

While bird monitoring technicians Nick Schleissman and Grace [Last Name], ease into the morning with a cup of coffee, the rest of the Bird Conservancy of the Rockies crew bustles around to prepare to leave for their ninth consecutive day on the job.

Schleissman, who has worked more field jobs than he can remember, said compared to the spring and summer months during the breeding season, waking up at 5 a.m. is considered “sleeping in” for his line of work.

“As soon as my alarm is going off before 3 a.m., that's it — I'm not doing it,” he said.

The group heads out around 6 a.m. for the Chihuahuan Desert grasslands about an hour north of Las Cruces. As they arrive at their work site, the sun has just started to break over the horizon.

Today, the group is looking for Chestnut-collared Longspur and Baird’s Sparrow — two key grassland bird species facing significant declines in their populations. Their goal is to safely capture, tag and release the target species in order to collect data that can be used for continued monitoring.

“We’re hoping to use the data that we collect here to inform bird conservation work because we’re trying to understand how populations are linked across the annual cycle,” Bird Conservancy of the Rockies research scientist Jessie Reese said.

“That [data] allows us to understand what pressures those birds are facing across different population segments and allows us to understand if things that are happening down here in New Mexico are having an undue influence on any one breeding population and vice versa.”

Action Pt. 1

For decades, biologists from Bird Conservancy have migrated with grassland birds down to New Mexico during the wintering seasons since 1996, in order to conduct bird surveys and gather habitat data. Despite this, still little was known about specific migratory patterns and behaviors of grassland birds, causing roadblocks for conservationists when attempting to implement successful conservation practices.  

In 2020, the organization launched its migratory ecology program, working with the Motus program to track birds across their full annual cycle in the Great Plains and Chihuahuan Desert regions.  

The Motus Wildlife Tracking System is a global collaborative network of automated radio telemetry stations that allows researchers to study the movements of small animals tagged with radio transmitters. After a bird is tagged, station antennas will detect and record when the bird passes by, uploading that detection to an online database thereafter.

The network is made up of over 1,000 stations operated by nearly 900 different organizations across the United States and Canada, allowing for highly collaborative research and data sharing.

Prior to 2020, there was a large lack of Motus stations in the Great Plains region. Researchers knew little about grassland bird long distance movements, migratory timing, stopover locations or survival along the Central Flyway. This knowledge gap posed problems for conservation organizations when trying to create effective conservation strategies and practices for declining species.

“Bird Conservancy has really helped the Motus Network grow in the Great Plains and the Chihuahuan Desert,” Reese said. In the past five years, the organization has installed over 100 radio towers and tagged almost 400 grassland birds.

In doing so, the organization has begun to address that knowledge gap and identify crucial locations for conservation.

[TK: where are these locations and what have they learned?]

Some of the key species that Bird Conservancy targets for Motus tagging are the Baird’s Sparrow, Sprague’s Pipit, Thick-Billed Longspur, Grasshopper Sparrow, Horned Lark and Chestnut-collared Longspur.

This is a Chestnut-collared Longspur.

After successfully catching the bird, the Bird Conservancy team records data on its age, sex, weight, muscle and fat content, wings, tail and feathers.

According to the North American Breeding Bird Survey, it is estimated that the Chestnut-collared Longspur population has declined 73 percent since 1967. Degradation and loss of breeding habitat has caused the species to disappear from much of its historical range.

“The work that we’re doing is important for conservation of birds like the Chestnut-collared Longspur because before we can go out and try to conserve or better manage habitat, we have to understand what is causing these bird populations to decline in the first place,” Reese said.

This Chestnut-collared Longspur is a young adult male, which the team was able to determine by examining the feather colors.

Once data on physical measurements is collected, the technicians attach a Motus tag that sits like a tiny, lightweight backpack between the bird’s wings.

Tagged and ready for monitoring, the bird is then safely released back into its habitat.

While the Bird Conservancy research team has specific target species in mind for each day’s work and location, sometimes spontaneity plays a role.

This is a Lapland Longspur.

Lapland Longspurs migrate all throughout North America, stretching as far north as Alaska where they can be found breeding in the arctic tundra. While their remote range has made them very difficult to track, it is believed that their populations have remained mostly stable over the last half century.

It was not one of the group’s target species, so they did not tag it. However, because it was beyond its expected wintering range, the research team still recorded data on the physical measurements of the bird to document the sighting.

“When we got that Lapland Longspur, someone said that's the first county record and we were all really excited about it,” bird monitoring technician Nick Schleissman said. “That was a little tiny moment where the birder in all of us came out because that wasn't one of our focal species — we were not expecting to get it.”

In 2020, the organization launched its migratory ecology program with the aim of developing a network of automated telemetry stations in the Great Plains and Chihuahuan Desert regions

Thirty years later,

[TK something like: The migratory patterns and behaviors of grassland birds have been relatively under researched, compared to other bird groups such as ducks and waterfowl. This has caused significant roadblocks ]

“We’re hoping to use the data that we collect here to inform bird conservation work because we’re trying to understand how populations are linked across the annual cycle,” Reese, who leads Bird Conservancy’s migratory ecology program, said.

“That [data] allows us to understand what pressures those birds are facing across different population segments and allows us to understand if things that are happening down here in New Mexico are having an undue influence on any one breeding population and vice versa.”

Data/Science

Over the past 50 years, scientists from U.S. bird conservation groups have been noticing an alarming trend: birds are rapidly vanishing across every North American biome. In 2019, a landmark study revealed that 3 billion breeding birds had been lost since 1970.

The findings set off a state of emergency throughout the conservation world, as scientists, policy makers and bird lovers started racing to figure out how to slow down an impending extinction crisis.

In the Great Plains, birds were disappearing at a rate higher than species in any other range, having lost more than half of their populations in the last 50 years.  

Six years later, the same remains true. Despite several efforts to curb losses, grassland birds continue to be the most imperiled species in North America, declining at a rate of 43 percent with some species like the Mountain Plover, Chestnut-collared Longspur and Baird’s Sparrow declining over 67 percent according to the 2025 State of the Birds report.

The main driver behind the widespread decline of birds in the heartland of America is habitat loss.

Despite being among the most endangered ecosystems in the U.S., grasslands are the least protected. According to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, 62 percent of the North American grassland biome has been lost as a result of agricultural conversion, development and the spread of invasive trees — resulting in hundreds of millions of acres of habitat loss for birds.

It is estimated that the Great Plains region loses 1 to 2 million acres of grasslands each year.

[TK: where are grasslands being lost more rapidly?]

A significant majority of birds that rely solely on grasslands for breeding are migratory, traveling long distances between their breeding and wintering grounds each year. Grasslands continue to shrink not only in places critical for breeding, but also in places needed to rest and refuel during migration and wintering months, further exacerbating bird decline.  

“The reality is that we can't restore all of the grassland that's been lost in North America. We have to eat. The Great Plains is really the breadbasket of North America,” Reese said. “We really need to understand where we should be doing conservation work — are there places that are going to be more beneficial where we'll get a bigger bang for our conservation buck?”

Habitat loss is not the only pressure impacting grassland bird populations. Climate change, pesticide usage and woody plant encroachment have also contributed to widespread declines. However, behind all of these threats is one common denominator: humans.

Importance

Without major intervention, several grassland bird species are on track to go extinct [by the end of the century].

Catastrophic losses in bird populations would dramatically alter and threaten the health of the Great Plains region, as birds provide vital ecological services to grasslands, such as pest control and seed dispersal.

As climate change triggers rising temperatures, shifts in precipitation patterns and more frequent extreme weather events such as drought and wildfires, loss of both birds and grasslands will continue to accelerate.

The implications of grassland loss extends further than just birds. With one-fourth of all U.S. crops produced in the Great Plains, declines in grassland health could mean large-scale disruptions in the country’s food supply and economy.

“Grasslands are the lifeblood of the central part of the U.S. and if they disappear, then this landscape will be drastically altered.” Brandt Ryder, chief conservation scientist at Bird Conservancy of the Rockies, said. “You can think of it like a big, giant Dust Bowl.”

Challenges

[TK: Section about how funding for bird conservation became volatile over the last year/how attacks on environmental protections by the Trump administration will impact birds]

Conservation has a long history of being a bipartisan issue in the United States.

However, climate and environment has become a target of the Trump administration in an effort to open up more federal land for fossil fuel production.

“Are we going into an extinction crisis? Well, if we don’t have conservation professionals and we don’t invest in it, then year, we likely will,” Ryder said.

“We’re between a rock and a hard place. We’re being asked to do the same or more with less resources,” Ryder said.

“It’s a bird challenge to try to do work as a bird conservation organization. To try to reverse or even stall bird populations declines, it’s just a really big problem. We do what we can by working together with other like-minded organizations and other agencies. But there’s a lot of really hard work that has to be done.” Jessie Reese

Action Pt. 2 - ‘What’s good for the bird is good for the herd.’

While ranching and farming have contributed to the loss of grasslands, it is also a big part of the solution moving forward.

“You will hear from a lot of folks that work on grassland bird conservation that ranching is an essential part of conserving grassland birds,” Ryder said. “We have pithy statements like, ‘what’s good for the bird is good for the herd’.”

Historically, grassland birds evolved on the landscape with large ungulates such as bison. Cattle serve the same purpose in terms of the impact that they have on the vegetation and habitat for grassland birds.

Sarah Sortum, fourth generation owner of Switzer Ranch located in Burwell, Nebraska, began implementing bird conservation practices on her land about 15 years ago.  

In addition to their cattle grazing operations, Sortum and her family started a bird watching tourism business on the ranch in [TK]. Upon discovering the plight of grassland birds soon after, they started implementing conservation practices, such as prescribed burning and woody tree mitigation, in an effort to maintain the birds’ prairie habitat.

<iframe title="vimeo-player" src="https://player.vimeo.com/video/696741118?h=7832bddb5b" width="640" height="360" frameborder="0" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allow="autoplay; fullscreen; picture-in-picture; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; web-share"   allowfullscreen></iframe>

After a while, Sortum started to notice that improving the health of the ecosystem didn’t only benefit the birds, but it also benefited their cattle operation as well.

“When we realized that we could easily change a few management practices that not only would benefit the birds, but benefit our bottom line on our cattle operation as well, it was a no-brainer.”

Despite the Covid-19 Pandemic causing them to suspend bird watching tours, Sortum and her family continued on with their bird conservation practices.

“I really see the birds on our place as my barometer for our overall resource health,” Sortum said. “For ​​farmers and ranchers — like any other family business — your hope and dream is that generations after you will benefit from what you've done and will be able to continue with the same opportunities. If we don't have a healthy resource, then that's going to be very challenging for them.”

Switzer Ranch is one of many ranches and farms in the Great Plains that has implemented bird-friendly practices on their ranch.

[TK: Audubon’s Conservation Ranching Program. 120 partners, adding up to over 3 million acres of ranch land.]

[TK: USDA Conservation Reserve Program]

Who Cares?

Restoring bird populations has wide ranging benefits that go beyond simply improving habitat health and the birds themselves. Birding is a big business.

According to the 2022 National Survey of Fishing, Hunting and Wildlife-Associated Recreation, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service estimated that bird-related activities generated almost $280 billion to the American economy and created 1.4 million jobs.

The survey also estimated that nearly 100 million Americans are bird watchers.

Chris Chappell became interested in birds at just 8 years old after he took care of a couple of chicks that fell out of a nest in his backyard.

A few years later, on a vacation with his family in Arizona, Chappell went on a field trip to a nearby wildlife refuge led by a wildlife biologist.

“We saw all these amazing birds,” Chappell said. “I still remember the American Avocet and the Black-necked stilt — looking at them through the scope. That was the moment I went from being a bird watcher, you might say, to being a birder.”

At age 15, he started leading Audubon field trips and Christmas Bird Counts in Washington state.

The National Audubon Society’s Christmas Bird Count started in 1900 and is the county’s longest running community science bird project. Since its mere beginnings over a century ago, thousands of volunteers across North and South America contribute to this wildlife census each year.

The Christmas Bird Count has enabled researchers and conservationists to study the long-term health and population trends of birds across the continent. Combined with other surveys such as the U.S. Geological Survey’s North American Breeding Bird Survey, started in 1966, the data has been vital for informing conservation strategies.

In 2002, the Cornell Lab of Ornithology launched its own participatory science program known as eBird. Since then, bird watchers worldwide have contributed to billions of bird observations that are compiled into a comprehensive database.  

Like the Christmas Bird Count, eBird has become an invaluable resource for conservation, helping scientists in better understanding bird abundance and distribution.

Without birders and hunters collecting data, many of the scientific discoveries in the bird world in the last half century might have been missed, including the documentation of widescale losses in North American bird populations.

Chappell — who is now 65-years-old and running his own bird watching tour business in Santa Fe, New Mexico — still regularly participates in Audubon’s Christmas Bird Count and is also recognized in the top 50 eBirders in the state.

The Future

[TK]
